EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An elongated area of low pressure moved across NY and New England during New Year's Day intensifying into a strong area of low pressure that stalled across Newfoundland on January 2nd and 3rd.||Initially there was a mix of snow and rain across northern VT on New Year's day with accumulating snow during the late evening into the morning hours of January 3rd (especially along the western slopes of the northern Green Mountains). The Champlain and Connecticut river valleys received a dusting to a few inches with the interior valleys receiving several inches and the higher western upslope terrain witnessing 6 to 12 inches and locally higher in Orleans county.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall in Orleans county ranged from 3 to 6 inches in eastern sections to over a foot along the Green Mountains and neighboring communities. Westfield reported 21 inches, 14 inches in Morgan, 13 inches in Greensboro with 9 inches in Craftsbury and Lowell.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large stationary storm in Newfoundland delivered an favorable flow of moisture and energy southwestward into northern NY and VT on January 7th-9th.||Snow overspread northern sections and was enhanced by the favorable NNW upslope of the Adirondacks and Green Mountains beginning on the morning of January 7th and continued intermittently until ending on January 9th.||Snowfall totals ranged from a dusting to 2 inches across the southern half of VT and the Connecticut River Valley with 2 to 4 inches in the immediate northern Champlain Valley and the valley floors of north central and northeast VT and 6 to 12 inches along the spine of the Green Mountains from Chittenden county to the Canadian border.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall ranged from 4 to 8 inches across the region.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak wave of low pressure developed and traveled along a stationary front draped across northern NY and VT during the evening of March 28th and continuing into the 29th.  Snowfall amounts ranged from a dusting to a few inches across southern VT to a half foot or more in the northern sections. Meanwhile across the southern tier there was mixed winter precipitation, including freezing rain and icing that accumulated upwards of an half inch in some locations. Power outages across northern VT, mainly central and southern VT were around 20000.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall totals of 4 to 8 inches were observed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Another clipper system moved across southern Vermont during the evening hours on January 28 through the early morning hours on January 30, 2025. Moderate snowfall accumulations occurred where snowfall totals ranged between 3 and 7 inches. Strong winds developed once again on January 29 with widespread gusts between 40 and 50 mph.||Key impacts: travel delays.

Southern Vermont was impacted by an arctic airmass during the early morning hours on January 22, 2025. The combination of below normal air temperatures, breezy winds, and nighttime radiational cooling led to minimum wind chill (or feels'-like temperatures) between -15 and -30 degrees Fahrenheit. The lowest of these air temperatures and wind chill values occurred at the highest elevations of the Southern Green Mountains.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Persistent upslope flow resulted in periods of snow across Bennington County in southern Vermont from January 1-5, 2025. The steadiest upslope snow occurred during the afternoon hours on January 1 into the early morning hours on January 2. Thereafter, occasional light snow and bursts of lake effect snow passed across the region into January 5 before snow ended. During this 5-day event, 6 to 12 inches of snow fell across Bennington County with the highest values across the southern Green Mountains. ||The Vermont Route 9 Chain-Up Law was activated between Bennington and Wilmington beginning in the early evening hours on January 1 until the early morning hours on January 2.||Key impacts: travel delays.

An upper level area of low pressure and a surface system that moved across southern Canada provided a moist, NNW upslope flow across the northern Green Mountains. Snow began around Midnight on January 14th and continued into the morning hours of January 15th.||Snowfall amounts ranged from a dusting to 2 inches from Addison to Orange counties southward with 2 to 5 inches in northeast Vermont and the northern Champlain valley as well as Washington county and 6 to 12 inches across the northwest facing slopes of the northern Green Mountains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall totals of 4 to 10 inches were observed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure tracking from the Carolinas northeastward off the East Coast brought a widespread moderate snowfall to southern Vermont on January 19-20, 2025. A weak area of low pressure and associated front moved across northern NY and Vermont during the afternoon and evening hours of May 17th. This system moved across a relatively unstable airmass across the region that resulted in numerous showers and thunderstorms.||Some of the thunderstorms were severe with damaging winds and large hail upward of golf ball size (1.75 inches diameter). In addition there was heavy rain and some training of thunderstorms that quickly produced 2 to 3 inches of rain. some of these areas had actually witnessed 1/2 to 1+ inches of rain the preceding day, making it more vulnerable to flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Golf ball size hail reported as well as other numerous reports of 1 inch or greater hail in Charlotte.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Weak low pressure moved from the Ohio River Valley on February 8th to off of Cape Cod Massachusetts ono the morning of February 9th. Snow overspread VT during the late evening of February 8th and ended around daybreak on February 9th.||It was a light, powdery snow that fell overnight on a weekend, leading to minimal impacts. Snowfall totals ranged from 1 to 2 inches along the Canadian border, 2 to 4 inches along the Route 2 corridor, 4 to 8 inches from Middlebury to Lake Morey with 6 to 10 inches across the southern four counties of VT.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall amounts across the region were 5 to 8 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure developed in the lower Mississippi River Valley on the evening of February 15th (Saturday) and then moved northeast across the Ohio River Valley midday on February 16th (Sunday)and into the Gulf of Maine during the evening hours.||A large pressure gradient led to strong southeast winds on the front end of the storm with cold and equally strong northwest winds after storm passage into Newfoundland.||Snow overspread the region late Saturday afternoon, snowing heavy at times during Sunday before ending Sunday night. Some sleet mixed in at times Sunday across northern and central area with a complete changeover to sleet and freezing rain with some ice accumulations in southern areas before changing back to snow during the afternoon and evening.||Scattered power outages were reported on February 17th due to strong winds in excess of 50 mph in northeast VT and in southern VT where it combined with some lightly ice laden trees.||Combination of strong winds in excess of 40 mph and deep snow led to a 100 foot barn collapse in the town of Williamstown. The end result was the barn was a total loss and at least 6 cows had died.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall totals across the county were 8 to 15 inches with brisk winds developing in the evening hours to begin blowing and drifting snow that caused some driving hazards of poor visibility and snow covered roads through February 17th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure developed in the lower Mississippi River Valley on the evening of February 15th (Saturday) and then moved northeast across the Ohio River Valley midday on February 16th (Sunday)and into the Gulf of Maine during the evening hours.||A large pressure gradient led to strong southeast winds on the front end of the storm with cold and equally strong northwest winds after storm passage into Newfoundland.||Snow overspread the region late Saturday afternoon, snowing heavy at times during Sunday before ending Sunday night. Some sleet mixed in at times Sunday across northern and central area with a complete changeover to sleet and freezing rain with some ice accumulations in southern areas before changing back to snow during the afternoon and evening.||Scattered power outages were reported on February 17th due to strong winds in excess of 50 mph in northeast VT and in southern VT where it combined with some lightly ice laden trees.||Combination of strong winds in excess of 40 mph and deep snow led to a 100 foot barn collapse in the town of Williamstown. The end result was the barn was a total loss and at least 6 cows had died.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system brought a period of snow changing to a wintry mix from south to north across southern Vermont during the morning hours on February 6, 2025. Snowfall amounts generally ranged from a coating to 3 inches with a light glaze of ice resulting in slippery travel conditions, especially during the morning commute. The Vermont Route 9 Chain-Up Law was activated between Bennington and Wilmington during this event.||Key impacts: travel delays.

Strong, westerly winds developed across southern Vermont behind the passage of a cold front as amplifying surface high pressure approached from the Ohio Valley and a low pressure system strengthened east of the New England Coast. Peak wind gusts across the region ranged from around 30 to 50 miles per hour with a peak gust of 53 miles per hour in the town of Bennington in Bennington County.

Low pressure developed in the lower Mississippi River Valley on the evening of February 15th (Saturday) and then moved northeast across the Ohio River Valley midday on February 16th (Sunday)and into the Gulf of Maine during the evening hours.||A large pressure gradient led to strong southeast winds on the front end of the storm with cold and equally strong northwest winds after storm passage into Newfoundland.||Snow overspread the region late Saturday afternoon, snowing heavy at times during Sunday before ending Sunday night. Some sleet mixed in at times Sunday across northern and central area with a complete changeover to sleet and freezing rain with some ice accumulations in southern areas before changing back to snow during the afternoon and evening.||Scattered power outages were reported on February 17th due to strong winds in excess of 50 mph in northeast VT and in southern VT where it combined with some lightly ice laden trees.||Combination of strong winds in excess of 40 mph and deep snow led to a 100 foot barn collapse in the town of Williamstown. The end result was the barn was a total loss and at least 6 cows had died.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous wind gusts in excess of 40 mph were recorded and combined with freshly fallen snow for considerable blowing and drifting snow that caused some driving hazards of poor visibility and snow covered roads.

An area of low pressure moved across Quebec trailing an associated cold front in which a weak area of low pressure developed and traveled across New England on the night of February 3rd.||Mixed rain and snow developed during the afternoon of February 3rd and changed to snow during the evening hours before ending during the early morning hours of February 4th. ||The initial rain and snow mixed, followed by falling temperatures and snow created an icy layer that resulted in treacherous roads during the night and early morning hours. EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall amounts across the area were generally 3 to 5 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak area of low pressure and associated front moved across northern NY and Vermont during the afternoon and evening hours of May 17th. This system moved across a relatively unstable airmass across the region that resulted in numerous showers and thunderstorms.||Some of the thunderstorms were severe with damaging winds and large hail upward of golf ball size (1.75 inches diameter). In addition there was heavy rain and some training of thunderstorms that quickly produced 2 to 3 inches of rain. some of these areas had actually witnessed 1/2 to 1+ inches of rain the preceding day, making it more vulnerable to flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Dime size hail reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak area of low pressure and associated front moved across northern NY and Vermont during the afternoon and evening hours of May 17th. This system moved across a relatively unstable airmass across the region that resulted in numerous showers and thunderstorms.||Some of the thunderstorms were severe with damaging winds and large hail upward of golf ball size (1.75 inches diameter). In addition there was heavy rain and some training of thunderstorms that quickly produced 2 to 3 inches of rain. some of these areas had actually witnessed 1/2 to 1+ inches of rain the preceding day, making it more vulnerable to flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Tree down on Berlin road as well a reports of other downed trees in the vicinity.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Multiple rounds of precipitation moved across southern Vermont on March 29-30, 2025. A wedge of cold air near the surface led to rounds of freezing rain. Flat ice accumulations in excess of 0.50 inches occurred in parts of the area resulting in high accretion on trees and power lines. Some trees and wires were downed resulting in some power outages.||Key impacts: travel delays, power outages.

A weak trough/cold front across the northern tier of NY and VT provided enough lift in a very hot and humid airmass to produce scattered thunderstorms with a few isolated pulse severe thunderstorms due to localized downburst.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several reports of trees down and on utility wires across Groton and neighboring communities.

Strong winds developed across southern Vermont in the wake of a cold front beginning in the afternoon hours on March 6, 2025 and continued into March 7, 2025. Widespread wind gusts between 40 and 55 mph were observed. Strong winds continued on March 8 but were not as strong with some peak gusts between 40 and 50 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 55 mph wind gust was recorded at a VTRANS Mesonet site near Peru.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The combination of a couple potent, upper-level disturbances and a pre-frontal surface trough inflicted scattered severe thunderstorms across southern Vermont in the evening hours of Saturday, May 17. Several reports of tree damage were relayed to NWS Albany, which led to the temporary closures of several roadways.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter reported an uprooted tree with several others snapped near the intersection of Route 9 and Woodford State Park Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A potent upper-level disturbance, together with a northward-tracking surface low from the Mid-Atlantic region brought light to moderate snowfall to southern Vermont from late April 11 through early afternoon April 12. The heaviest snowfall was confined to the highest elevations of the Southern Green Mountains, though accumulations of one inch to 3 inches were common throughout Bennington and Windham Counties. Much of this fell during the overnight period of April 11 and, as this occurred over a weekend, little impact to travel occurred, though slippery road conditions did occur. Additionally, patches of light freezing drizzle lingered into the evening hours on April 12 leading to icy roadways and reports of a few vehicles skidding off the road. ||Key impacts: slippery travel conditions, minor traffic accidents.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Zone average snowfall of 3 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A dome of high pressure brought a period of very hot and humid conditions across southern Vermont from June 23-25, 2025. Peak heat index values (or feels-like temperatures) were between 95 and 104 degrees Fahrenheit in many locations. The highest heat index values (feels-like temperatures) occurred on June 23 with values between 105 and 110 degrees Fahrenheit, especially along the Connecticut River Valley. Preliminary data from the U.S. Health and Human Services showed that eight-year records for one day heat-related emergency department visits were set during this heat wave across region 1 which included all of Vermont and the rest of New England.

A complex winter storm impacted southern Vermont between February 15th and 16th, 2025 bringing snow, freezing rain, and strong winds. Snow accumulations ranged from nearly 7 to 10 inches across Bennington and Windham Counties with flat ice accreting up to 0.20 to 0.25 inches. By the event's conclusion, the total liquid equivalent across southern Vermont ranged from about 1.15 to 1.25 inches. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level area of high pressure, this time of the year called a heat dome, was centered across the Appalachians/Mid-Atlantic states ridging across the northeast including NY and New England. ||This airmass on June 23rd was considerably hot and humid with temperatures in the 90s and oppressive dewpoints in the low to mid 70s. Heat index values ranged from the mid-upper 90s across northeast VT to the 100 to 110 range across the Connecticut river and Champlain valleys.||Temperatures were only a few degrees lower for most of VT on June 24th but still  dangerous hot with heat index values mainly between 95 and 110. Health related impacts were not available.||Here were temperature records for the three recognized climatological sites (Burlington, Montpelier and St. Johnsbury) in VT.||June 23rd:|Burlington - 99 degrees surpassed the daily record and ties for the 4th Warmest ALL-TIME.|Montpelier - 93 degrees surpassed the daily record. |St. Johnsbury - 96 degrees surpassed the daily record.|Other observations showed mid to upper 90s across the region.||June 24th:|Burlington - 97 degrees surpassed the daily record.|Montpelier - 93 degrees surpassed the daily record. |St. Johnsbury - 96 degrees surpassed the daily record.|Other observations showed mid to upper 90s across the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Dangerous heat across the region for both June 23rd and 24th with daytime temperatures in the lower to mid 90s and dewpoints in the 70s creating heat index values between 95 and 105.

Immediately on the heels of a complex winter storm that impacted southern Vermont on February 15-16th, 2025, strong winds and bitter cold broke out across Bennington and Windham Counties. Strongest wind gusts ranged from around 40 mph to 60 mph with a peak wind gust of 69 mph recorded near the town of Peru in Bennington County. The cold airmass inflicted across southern Vermont on the departing side of the storm of the days prior led to wind chill values anywhere from -1 to -18.||Key Impacts: power outages.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak trough/cold front across the northern tier of NY and VT provided enough lift in a very hot and humid airmass to produce scattered thunderstorms with a few isolated pulse severe thunderstorms due to localized downburst.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered, slow moving thunderstorms developed across portions of south central Vermont on the afternoon of June 6, 2025 as a slow moving front sagged southward through the region. A few of the storms produced localized heavy rainfall of between 2 and 3.5 inches, leading to localized reports of flooding in Windsor County, VT.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Water flooded several sections of Route 106 near Amsden, VT as reported by the public and law enforcement.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak cold front slowly moved across northern NY and VT during the afternoon and evening hours of July 10th. Several rounds of tropical showers and thunderstorms traversed the area with a few storms pulsing to severe limits and localized excessive rainfall (4-5+ inches) in NE VT leading to localized flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several reports of trees and other wind damage debris as well as partial roof torn of the  Middlebury High School. Photography and radar evidence of a prolonged, strong, wet microburst.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A ridge of high pressure brought a period of hot and humid conditions across eastern Windham County in southern Vermont on July 16-17, 2025. Maximum heat index values (feels-like temperatures) between 95 and 102 degrees occurred each afternoon.

A ridge of high pressure brought a period of hot and humid conditions across eastern Windham County in southern Vermont on July 16-17, 2025. Maximum heat index values (feels-like temperatures) between 95 and 102 degrees occurred each afternoon.

A weak cold front slowly moved across northern NY and VT during the afternoon and evening hours of July 10th. Several rounds of tropical showers and thunderstorms traversed the area with a few storms pulsing to severe limits and localized excessive rainfall (4-5+ inches) in NE VT leading to localized flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: One inch diameter hail observed and photographed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A pre-frontal trough moved across the northern Champlain Valley during the afternoon hours of July 17th into a hot and moderately unstable airmass, but lacked organized upper level winds. Numerous showers and thunderstorms moved across the region, but only one storm for a brief period of time, likely produced a wet microburst that knocked down a few trees onto wires and a structure causing minor damage in Swanton.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Tree, wire, and minor structural damage to a home in Swanton.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moved across a moderately unstable airmass across northern NY and VT during the afternoon and early evening hours on July 3rd. Several broken lines of thunderstorms moved across northern NY into VT, some producing damaging winds or hail of one inch in diameter or greater. Approximately 10,000 customer were left without power.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Tree down into powerlines on West Shore Road in South Hero/Keeler Bay.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered showers and thunderstorms developed ahead of an approaching cold front on July 20, 2025. One storm became severe across southern Vermont downing a tree on a house near Saxtons River.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was downed on a house in Saxtons River.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak cold front slowly moved across northern NY and VT during the afternoon and evening hours of July 10th. Several rounds of tropical showers and thunderstorms traversed the area with a few storms pulsing to severe limits and localized excessive rainfall (4-5+ inches) in NE VT leading to localized flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Localized torrential rainfall from 2 to 5 inches affected portions of Caledonia County during the afternoon/early evening of July 10, 2025, particularly in the West Burke area. Several reports of road/culvert washouts were received via social media and news reports.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak cold front slowly moved across northern NY and VT during the afternoon and evening hours of July 10th. Several rounds of tropical showers and thunderstorms traversed the area with a few storms pulsing to severe limits and localized excessive rainfall (4-5+ inches) in NE VT leading to localized flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall from slow moving thunderstorms affected portions of Essex County, VT, particularly in the East Haven area, during the afternoon/early evening of July 10, 2025. Several reports of road/culvert washouts were received.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak cold front slowly moved across northern NY and VT during the afternoon and evening hours of July 10th. Several rounds of tropical showers and thunderstorms traversed the area with a few storms pulsing to severe limits and localized excessive rainfall (4-5+ inches) in NE VT leading to localized flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Isolated heavy rainfall from slow moving thunderstorms affected portions of Addison County, VT, particularly in the New Haven area, during the afternoon/early evening of July 10, 2025. Radar estimates and reliable rain gauge observations showed up to 3 inches of rain fell in this area, leading to a few reports of high water and/or road closures.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High pressure over the region brought a period of hot and humid conditions to eastern Windham County in southern Vermont on July 29, 2025. Maximum heat index values (feels like temperatures) between 95 and 100 degrees was observed.

A weak cold front slowly moved across northern NY and VT during the afternoon and evening hours of July 10th. Several rounds of tropical showers and thunderstorms traversed the area with a few storms pulsing to severe limits and localized excessive rainfall (4-5+ inches) in NE VT leading to localized flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Localized torrential rainfall from slow moving thunderstorms fell across portions of Caledonia County, VT on the afternoon/early evening of July 10, 2025. Radar estimates and reliable rain gauge observations show that between 2 and 5 inches of rain fell in areas around Sutton and Burke, VT where a number of flash flooding reports were received. Numerous road washouts occurred in this area, with at least one water rescue needed and several homes damaged along Calendar Brook Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Hot and humid conditions ahead of an upper level disturbance and surface boundary generated enough instability to drive the development of scattered thunderstorms across southern Vermont on the afternoon of August 13, 2025.  One storm in particular reached severe magnitude, inflicting wind damage to trees and wires along North Road at Beacon Hollow Road and Sunderland Hill Road near Arlington.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An Amateur Radio operator reported a tree and wires down on Sunderland Hill Road near the Elementary School.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong surface low in Canada between James Bay and the Great Lakes traversed Ontario and Quebec provinces on December 19th. This storm pushed a cold front across NY and VT during the morning hours with strong, gusty and damaging southwest-west winds in excess of 60 mph in several locations in northern VT resulting in damaged trees, utility lines and scattered power outages of up to 25,000 customers.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Scattered tree damage with several measured wind gusts in excess of 60 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A fast moving clipper system and cold front moved across NY and New England on December 7th. Snow quickly overspread during the afternoon hours and exited before midnight. A widespread 3 to 6 inches of snow was observed in northern sections, 2 to 5 inches in central sections and a dusting to 2 inches in southern areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall totals of a 1 to 2 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Following dry conditions during the month of September, continued spells of dry weather during the month of October led to the continuation of severe drought (D2) per the U.S. Drought Monitor across much of Southern Vermont, including Bennington and Windham Counties. Severe drought continued into the month of November.

Strong winds developed ahead of a potent frontal system in the highest terrain of the Southern Green Mountains on October 30th, 2025. The strongest gust was recorded in Bennington County with an isolated magnitude of 53 mph. Elsewhere, wind gusts ranged from about 20-40 mph.

A very potent low pressure system brought widespread strong winds to much of southern Vermont on Halloween (October 31), 2025. Widespread gusts of 40 to 50 mph were common, especially during the evening and late evening hours.

Developing and strengthening low pressure across the Great Lakes on December 28th moved across Ontario and Quebec provinces on December 29th. Mild, moist flow accompanying this system moved into cold, dense low level cold air entrenched across northern NY and VT.||Precipitation mainly fell as freezing rain during the late evening hours of December 28th into the morning hours of December 29th. This led to very hazardous driving conditions with numerous vehicle accidents as well as enough ice accretion on trees to cause some isolated to scattered power outages (approximately 10,000) . Elevated flat ice accumulations across VT was generally 0.1 to 0.25 inches with amounts approaching 0.5 inches across the mountain valleys of central VT.||More than 100 vehicle accidents reported by the VT State Police alone.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Elevated flat ice accumulations were generally 1/4 to 1/2 of an inch across the area with vehicle accidents being the main impact and some isolated to scattered power outages.

This led to very hazardous driving conditions with numerous vehicle accidents as well as enough ice accretion on trees to cause some isolated to scattered power outages (approximately 10,000) . Elevated flat ice accumulations across VT was generally 0.1 to 0.25 inches with amounts approaching 0.5 inches across the mountain valleys of central VT.||More than 100 vehicle accidents reported by the VT State Police alone.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Elevated flat ice accumulations were generally 0.1 to 0.25 inches across the area with vehicle accidents being the main impact.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A developing coastal low pressure off the North Carolina coast slowly moved northeast toward the 40 degree North/70 degree West benchmark on December 2nd. Light snow overspread Vermont from south to north around daybreak and exited during the evening hours from west to east.||Snowfall totals ranged from 1 to 3 inches in northwest VT and 5 to 8 inches across southern and eastern VT. This impacted both the morning and afternoon-evening commutes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall totals across the region were 5 to 8 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: In the wake of the freezing rain/ice storm, a cold front crossed the region bringing a return to colder conditions as well as strong winds from the afternoon hours on December 29 through the evening hours on December 30, 2025. Widespread wind gusts on the order of 40 to 50 mph were observed with a peak wind gust of 60 mph at a mesonet site near Peru.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 60 mph wind gust was recorded at a Vermont Mesonet site near Peru. Other widespread wind gusts between 45 and 55 mph were observed across the county.

A complex, relatively progressive winter storm impacted southern Vermont in the preliminary days of December, bringing widespread moderate to heavy snow particularly to the highest elevations of the Southern Green Mountains. ||An initial area of snow ahead of the main stratiform precipitation shield tracked through the region in the early morning hours of December 2, leading to a quick accumulation of a trace up to 1 inch in some isolated areas. The main precipitation shield then moved in just before 7 AM, bringing moderate to heavy snowfall rates in areas where enhanced forcing created localized, embedded mesoscale snow bands. Snow concluded late December 2/early December 3 with total accumulations ranging from about 2 to 11.5 inches. ||Being that this an early season storm, it acted as the first snowfall of the season, leading to numerous traffic accidents, school closures and delays, and travel delays.  ||Key Impacts: Traffic accidents, travel delays, school closures, school delays.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Zone average snowfall of 6.2 inches and widespread impacts including closed schools and motor vehicle accidents.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong clipper system impacted southern Vermont on December 10, bringing moderate to heavy snow across the high elevations of the Southern Green Mountains and light to locally moderate accumulations in lower-lying areas. The heaviest snow not only resulted from the adjacency to the best forcing from the storm, but localized terrain enhancement. Within the Southern Green Mountains, anywhere from about 6 to nearly 10 inches of snow accumulated. Elsewhere, accumulations of about 1 to 4 inches were common. ||Key impacts: traffic accidents, travel delays.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Zone average snowfall of 6.1 inches with accumulations across the higher terrain of 6 to 9 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Sub-freezing temperatures ahead of an approaching warm front resulted in a period of accumulating freezing rain for all of southern Vermont during the evening hours on December 28, 2025 into the overnight or morning hours on December 29, 2025. As temperatures rose above freezing, precipitation eventually changed to plain rain for many areas. Flat ice accumulations of a light glaze to 0.25 inches occurred which resulted in some slippery travel conditions and isolated power outages.

Another complex winter storm impacted southern Vermont, this time just after Christmas, December 26-27, 2025. After days worth of storm track shifting in the guidance, the resulting more northerly track of the storm kept Bennington and Windham Counties on the outskirts of the heaviest area of snowfall. Across the highest elevations of the Southern Green Mountains, accumulations of 4 to 6 inches were largely reported. However, in the lower terrain, only about 2.5 to 3.5 inches accumulated in most areas. ||Key impacts: traffic accidents, travel delays.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Zone average snowfall of 4.6 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low pressure system tracking from the Upper Great Lakes into Quebec brought a period of rainfall and strong winds to southern Vermont from the evening hours on December 18 to the early morning hours on December 20, 2025. Winds started out southerly from the evening hours December 18 into the morning hours on December 19 before shifting to the west during the afternoon and evening hours on December 19 behind the passage of a strong cold front. Widespread wind gusts on the order of 40 to 50 mph were observed both ahead and behind the cold front with a peak wind gust of 60 mph measured at the Bennington State Airport.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 60 mph wind gust was recorded at the Bennington State Airport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low pressure system tracking from the Upper Great Lakes into Quebec brought a period of rainfall and strong winds to southern Vermont from the evening hours on December 18 to the early morning hours on December 20, 2025. Winds started out southerly from the evening hours December 18 into the morning hours on December 19 before shifting to the west during the afternoon and evening hours on December 19 behind the passage of a strong cold front. Widespread wind gusts on the order of 40 to 50 mph were observed both ahead and behind the cold front with a peak wind gust of 60 mph measured at the Bennington State Airport.

A weak, but complex area of low pressure traveled from the Mid-Atlantic Appalachians on November 9th across eastern New England during the afternoon and evening of November 10th. ||Precipitation started as a cold rain (temperatures in the 30s to around 40) during the 10th. As low pressure tracked across eastern New England and slightly strengthened during the late afternoon and evening rain changed to snow, heavy at times during the evening commute. This accounted for numerous (dozens and dozens) vehicle accidents across the Champlain Valley. Highest snowfall totals were 4 to 7 inches in the Champlain Valley of Grand Isle, Franklin and Chittenden counties quickly dropping off to 2 to 4 inches in neighboring counties.||Light snow and persistent snow showers fell during the late afternoon and evening commute on November 11th. Snowfall amounts were generally less than one inch but with falling temperatures below freezing and untreated roads...dozens of vehicle accidents were reported with numerous road closures. Impacts were possibly greater than the November 10th snowfall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall totals of 3 to 5 inches were observed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Spells of wet weather, beginning in October, allowed for severe drought (D2) conditions to end across Bennington County on November 4, 2025 and Windham County on November 11, 2025 per the U.S. Drought Monitor.

Windy conditions developed in the wake of a cold front on November 16, 2025. Peak wind gusts up to 45 mph were observed across portions of southern Vermont. In addition, upslope snow into November 17, 2025 brought moderate accumulations on the order of 3 to 7 inches to parts of Bennington and western Windham counties, especially in the southern Green Mountains.||Key impacts: transportation delays.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 45 mph wind gust was recorded at an observation site near Peru.

An area of low pressure across New England during the morning hours of November 16th moved into Newfoundland by November 17th and continued to exit the region. ||A counterclockwise, cold, unstable flow across the mountains of northern Vermont allowed for a orographic, upslope snowfall event that began in the northern Green Mountains during the afternoon of November 16th and ended during the afternoon of the 17th.||Snowfall was largely confined to the higher elevations and favored northwest upslope regions with 3 to 6 inches of snow but snowfall totals exceeding 10 inches were observed in the highest elevations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall totals ranged from 4 to 10+ inches across the region with the highest totals in the higher elevations.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall totals ranged from 4 to 10+ inches across the region with the highest totals in the higher elevations of western Orleans county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A frontal boundary stalled to the south of the ALY County Warning Area, in tandem with several mid- and upper-level disturbances generated widespread heavy rainfall and scattered thunderstorms across southern Vermont on June 6 and 7. Prolonged, moderate to locally heavy downpours lead to isolated poor drainage flooding in Windham County, with one report of a washout on Westminister W. Road. Over the course of the two days, rainfall totals amounted to two to two and a half inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A local CoCoRaHS observer reported washout conditions along Westminster W. Rd.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The passage of a strong cold front led to windy conditions, especially across the higher terrain of the Southern Green Mountains. Wind gusts anywhere from 20 to 40 mph were common across all of southern Vermont, however, with a peak gust of 47 mph recorded by a Road Weather Information Station along Interstate 91 in Rockingham in Windham County.

Following dry conditions during the month of August, continued spells of dry weather during the month of September led to the expansion of severe drought (D2) per the U.S. Drought Monitor across Bennington and Windham counties in southern Vermont. Severe drought continued into the month of October.
